# Projects

::: callout-caution
## Incomplete

I am currently transfer here in a consistent manner the public presentations and reports of the past 10 years. You can find a lot more on [https://reprex.nl/](https://reprex.nl/p) and <https://danielantal.eu/>.  I am planning to finish the migration by 21 June 2026.
:::


## Prospective project ideas

### PROCLAIM {#sec-proclaim}

[![Provenance-Aware Claims and Machine-Readable Rights](png/projects/proclaim.png){fig-alt="Provenance-Aware Claims and Machine-Readable Rights" fig-align="center"}](https://reprex.nl/project/proclaim/)

Journalistic archives introduce a semantic stabilisation problem that extends beyond traditional copyright management. While copyright infrastructures typically focus on identifying works, rightsholders, licences, and permitted uses, journalistic content also contains time-bound claims about events, persons, organisations, and public affairs.

A single journalistic article may simultaneously contain copyright-protected expression, references to identifiable persons, quotations from third parties, public records, freedom-of-information disclosures, and statements whose interpretation depends on historical and political context. As articles are reused, translated, summarised, linked, or processed by AI systems, the semantic relationship between these elements may become unstable.

The proposed `Provenance-Aware Claims and Machine-Readable Rights` ([proclaim](https://reprex.nl/project/proclaim/)) project investigates how semantic stabilisation methods can be applied to journalistic knowledge systems. The objective is to create machine-actionable representations linking articles, statements, events, actors, provenance assertions, and rights expressions. These representations would support lawful reuse, cross-border editorial collaboration, and AI-mediated information environments while preserving contextual integrity.

From the perspective of semantic stabilisation, PAJRA investigates a transition from entity stabilisation to claim stabilisation. The central question is not merely whether two identifiers refer to the same person or work, but whether two statements, potentially created in different languages, jurisdictions, or political contexts, support equivalent interpretations and inferences.

The project also serves as an experimental environment for studying provenance-aware rights expression, machine-readable policy frameworks such as ODRL, and the interaction between copyright, data protection, personality rights, editorial constraints, and public-interest exceptions. In this sense, journalistic archives provide a particularly demanding test case for semantic stabilisation because legal, semantic, and ethical constraints must remain aligned throughout the lifecycle of information.

### OpenSync {#sec-opensync}

The planned `OpenSynch` project addresses a structural gap in the cultural and creative sectors: the lack of interoperable, machine-readable metadata and rights information that prevents large parts of European music and audiovisual repertoires from being accessible and reusable.

The project develops and validates a workflow layer that enables continuous synchronisation of metadata and rights information across stakeholders (archives, CMOs, producers, distributors), supporting the identification, clarification, and activation of works and recordings.

flow layer that enables continuous synchronisation of metadata and rights The objective is not to create a new platform, but to make existing infrastructures work together, such as CMOs, archives, the Orphan works and Out-of-Commerce works and CommonsDB through interoperable, machine-actionable data and workflows aligned with CITF principles.

**Alignment with the CITF requirements**: OpenSynch directly tests and implements several requirements from the CITF First Project (Annex 3), in particular: 
-  Machine-readable rights expression: development of structured, incremental representations aligned with CITF directions 
-  Interoperability across systems: enabling exchange of copyright-relevant data between heterogeneous infrastructures 
-  Linking works, recordings, and contributors: improving identification and relationship modelling across sectors 
- Workflow integration: embedding rights data into operational processes (catalogue onboarding, distribution, reuse) The project focuses on practical implementation of these requirements in real-world workflows rather than theoretical modelling.

## Current projects

### FinFAIR (Awarded Projects from the First Call – Data in Echoes-ECCCH)

Finno-Ugric Dataspace: interconnecting the tangible and intangible heritage of regional identity groups under the framework of ECCCH

### OpenHerit (Awareded Projects from the Second Call in Echoes-ECCCH)

OpenHerit helps small and medium-sized cultural heritage institutions—particularly open-air museums and regional heritage organisations—prepare for participation in the European Collaborative Cloud for Cultural Heritage (ECCCH). Building on the RETOLD documentation framework, the project investigates how existing descriptions of buildings, crafts, traditions, and community heritage can be progressively aligned with emerging European interoperability frameworks such as CIDOC CRM and the Heritage Digital Twin Ontology (HDTO).

Rather than developing new software infrastructure, the project focuses on capacity building, documentation review, and practical workflows that enable museum professionals and volunteers to understand how their existing collections and documentation practices relate to the ECCCH ecosystem. Through workshops, guidance materials, and demonstrator examples, OpenHerit explores realistic pathways for creating simple heritage digital twins using existing infrastructures such as Wikimedia Commons, Wikidata, Europeana, and EOSC-aligned repositories.

From the perspective of semantic stabilisation, OpenHerit investigates how heterogeneous heritage documentation can be clarified, reviewed, and translated into interoperable semantic representations without imposing unrealistic technical requirements on smaller institutions. The project therefore serves as a practical testbed for transforming local documentation practices into reusable and trustworthy digital heritage knowledge.

### Unlabel

To be continued in @sec-opensync and following from @sec-openmuse, with Swedish
music distributor we are working on ways to read, stabilise and translate metadata
from corporate archives, and even public archives that help rights clearance and 
distribution to the public from studio or label archives to platforms like 
Spotify, Apple, Amazon. This work supports catalogue transfers for currently 
not available, or not well distributed music.

### Andrássy Archives {#sec-project-andrassy}

The Collection of the _Gyula Andrássy Foundation_ contains approximately two million digital objects representing archival records, photographs, maps, manuscripts, and related cultural heritage materials. Creating a traditional archival description for a collection of this size would require tens of millions of individual metadata statements and, under conventional workflows, hundreds of archivist-years of work. The project therefore addresses a fundamental challenge facing many large-scale cultural heritage collections: how to make vast digital holdings discoverable, researchable, and interoperable when comprehensive manual description is not economically feasible.

The proposed solution is a multi-stage semantic stabilisation workflow that combines persistent identification, automated metadata extraction, provenance-aware knowledge graphs, and human expert review. Rather than attempting to create complete descriptions immediately, the system progressively transforms partially known digital objects into increasingly rich semantic objects. Artificial intelligence assists in identifying document types, languages, dates, names, and inconsistencies, while archivists and researchers validate, refine, and enrich the resulting descriptions. The workflow explicitly recognises different levels of confidence, allowing collections to become searchable and usable long before complete scholarly description is achieved.

The project serves as a real-world laboratory for semantic stabilisation in digital humanities and cultural heritage. It explores how archival, library, and museum materials can be connected across institutions, languages, and descriptive traditions using Linked Open Data principles and knowledge graph technologies. Beyond the Andrássy collection itself, the project investigates how large-scale cultural heritage collections can be transformed from isolated digital files into contextualised and interoperable knowledge systems that support both human scholarship and machine-assisted discovery.


### Hungarian Motion Picture Data Sharing Space

## Past projects

### OpenMuse {#sec-openmuse}

![](png/projects/openmuse_2026.png)

Reprex initiated and carried out the largest part of this three-year and many stakeholder Horizon Europe Research and Innovation Action, with leadership in one Work Packages and several tasks in other work packages. 

OpenMusE brought  together music industry stakeholders and researchers from 11 EU countries and Ukraine. Our consortium recognises that placing European music ecosystems on a more competitive, fair, and sustainable footing requires evidence-based policymaking, business planning and accuracy. We provide the data needed for these actions.

Using transparent methods and tools, OpenMusE maps the policy as well as the data landscape. The project bridges data gaps and empowers stakeholders and policymakers to take data-driven actions. Our project is grounded on principles of open policy analysis, open science, and open-source software development. We work with stakeholders to identify data gaps on the EU, national, and regional levels; co-create indicators and methods for bridging them; develop free software tools for data collection and analysis; and report our findings and every step taken to reach them.

Using the OMO ([Open Music Observatory](https://openmusicobservatory.eu/)) and our open-source software, music SMEs without technical departments or expertise will be able to access and analyse open data; model volume and value, including of zero-price uses; create better business models; and generate corporate social responsibility and sustainability reports; all at a fraction of current costs. We validate these tools in four pilot studies that will bring concrete benefits to stakeholders within the project lifespan.

### Music Eviota {#sec-music-eviota}

The Eviota project aims to develop sustainability reports directly linked to the financial accounts of companies, NGOs, and civil society organisations. The initial phase focuses on estimating greenhouse gas emissions and air pollutants. Our goal is to generate reliable, expenditure-based estimates of the carbon and pollution footprint of music-related social enterprises—an approach we refer to as connected financial and sustainability reporting.or double materiality reporting.

The objective of the MusicAIRE GREEN Recovery Programme was to increase environmental sustainability and ecological awareness within the music sector. Its focus is on greening the industry—particularly live performances, festivals, and touring—while also supporting innovative start-ups that seek to reduce the environmental impact of online data storage and music distribution.

The Music Eviota project was supported by MusicAIRE. The MusicAIRE grant provided us with resources to support additional music businesses, civil society organisations, and researchers with high-quality data—free of charge during the project’s duration.

[Music Eviota](https://reprex.nl/project/musiceviota/)

### Surveyharmonies

Surveyharmonies was developed as a practical framework for increasing the value of survey research through semantic harmonisation and data reuse. The project emerged from a simple observation: many surveys collect information that is conceptually comparable to information already gathered in previous studies, other countries, or other research programmes, yet these data remain disconnected because their semantic correspondence has not been established.

Traditional survey practice treats each fieldwork exercise as a largely self-contained activity. Surveyharmonies approaches survey data as a cumulative knowledge resource. By harmonising concepts, variables, coding schemes, and metadata across independently collected surveys, a single fieldwork exercise can be enriched with observations collected elsewhere and at other times. This allows researchers and organisations to place local observations into broader comparative contexts without repeating expensive data collection efforts.

The methodological foundation of Surveyharmonies is semantic stabilisation. Before survey responses can be compared, combined, or reused, it is necessary to establish whether variables recorded under different names, languages, coding schemes, or institutional contexts represent sufficiently similar concepts. The project therefore combines survey harmonisation, metadata standardisation, codebook reconciliation, and linked data publication into a single workflow.

In practical applications, Surveyharmonies has been used to integrate cultural participation surveys, music consumption surveys, audiovisual sector research, and international comparative survey programmes. By connecting newly collected responses with harmonised historical and international survey data, researchers can move beyond simple local measurements and investigate temporal change, regional differences, and broader comparative patterns.

From the perspective of semantic stabilisation, Surveyharmonies represents one of the earliest practical implementations of the research programme. It demonstrated that semantic correspondence among independently collected observations could be established systematically and reused across projects, thereby transforming isolated survey datasets into cumulative and interoperable knowledge resources.

The project also provided many of the practical challenges that later motivated the development of the retroharmonize and dataset packages, particularly in the areas of concept harmonisation, metadata standardisation, provenance documentation, and reproducible survey integration.


## Listen Local Lithuania
